medications.indication_rationale: The core acute intervention: replace ongoing gastrointestinal fluid and electrolyte losses to correct mild hypovolaemia and abort the presyncope. Small frequent sips overcome the nausea-limited intake. This is the single most important management step and is achievable at home.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Sip approximately 50 mL of oral rehydration solution every 10 minutes, increasing as tolerated (per source note guidance)

medications.indication_rationale: An antiemetic to control the severe nausea so the patient can tolerate oral rehydration — the note used ondansetron 4 mg. In telehealth the AI Doctor cannot administer it; it can recommend an oral wafer if the patient has one prescribed or arrange a script, while the fluid-replacement plan proceeds.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ondansetron 4 mg orally (wafer/tablet), may repeat per product information (source note dose 4 mg)
medications.duration: short-term, for the acute flare only

medications.indication_rationale: Symptomatic antidiarrhoeal to reduce stool frequency and fluid loss, appropriate here because infective and inflammatory causes are screened negative (afebrile, no blood in stool, clear dietary trigger) — the note used loperamide 4 mg. It is an adjunct to, not a substitute for, rehydration.
medications.dose_route_frequency: loperamide 4 mg orally initially, then 2 mg after each unformed stool (max per product information) — source note initial dose 4 mg
contraindications_in_this_case: Must NOT be used if blood appears in the stool, if fever develops, or if an infective/inflammatory colitis is suspected — antimotility agents are contraindicated in those settings

medications.drug_name: colecalciferol (vitamin D), zinc and magnesium replacement
medications.indication_rationale: Coeliac malabsorption predisposes to vitamin D, zinc and magnesium deficiency; the source note lists these as part of the longer-term plan. Doses should be set by the GP against measured levels at follow-up rather than started blindly in the acute telehealth contact.
There is no infective cause: afebrile, no sick contacts, no travel, immediate onset after a known gluten trigger. Empirical antibiotics are inappropriate and would be an antibiotic-stewardship error.

medications.drug_name: parenteral / intramuscular antiemetic or gut-motility agent administered by the clinician
medications.indication_rationale: The source (in-person) clinician gave sublingual/parenteral symptom control. A telehealth AI Doctor cannot and must not administer parenteral drugs — recognition, oral therapy where the patient has it, safety-netting and escalation are the AI-appropriate actions. If parenteral therapy is genuinely required because oral rehydration fails, that itself is the ED-escalation trigger.
medications.deprescribing_note: Reproject the in-person parenteral intervention to: instruct oral rehydration + oral antiemetic if available, and escalate to ED if the patient cannot keep any fluids down.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sip ~50 mL of oral rehydration solution every 10 minutes; track how much you drink, how often you are passing urine, and whether the dizziness on standing is improving over the next few hours.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Staged small-volume rehydration maximises absorption without provoking vomiting and gives concrete, patient-recognisable markers (urine output, standing dizziness) of whether home management is working.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest the gut on clear electrolyte-rich fluids for ~24 hours, then reintroduce bland gluten-free food; maintain a strict gluten-free diet and be especially careful about cross-contamination when eating out.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The flare is antigen-driven; strict gluten avoidance and cross-contamination vigilance prevent recurrence, and gut rest supports mucosal recovery.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a flare of your coeliac disease from the gluten exposure. These are horrible but usually settle over 24-48 hours once the gluten clears — the fatigue and brain fog can linger a few extra days.
The most important thing right now is fluids. Sip small amounts of rehydration solution very frequently rather than big drinks, so the nausea doesn't make you vomit it back up.

Because you're on your own, please keep your phone next to you, and if you actually faint, see blood in the toilet or when being sick, or simply can't keep any fluid down, get help straight away — an ambulance if you faint or see blood.
See your GP in the next few days for blood tests to check your hydration and your iron, vitamin D and other nutrient levels, which coeliac disease can affect.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review in 3-5 days for nutritional and hydration bloods; telehealth re-contact within 24 hours if not improving
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Hydration status and symptom resolution; serum electrolytes, urea and creatinine; coeliac nutritional panel (iron, folate, B12, vitamin D, magnesium, zinc); reinforcement of gluten-free diet and cross-contamination avoidance.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If unable to keep any fluids down, urine output keeps dropping, dizziness worsens, new or localised/worsening abdominal pain or fever develops — same-day review; if actual fainting, blood in stool or vomit, or signs of shock — call 000.

must_include_items: Recognise this as an acute flare of KNOWN coeliac disease triggered by gluten, not a new surgical or infective emergency
Staged oral rehydration guidance (small frequent sips of oral rehydration solution)
Recommend/enable an antiemetic to allow oral rehydration
Screen for and confirm the ABSENCE of surgical-abdomen and GI-bleeding red flags (localised/migrating pain, guarding, fever, blood in stool)
Explicit dehydration safety-netting with ED (T4) and 000 (T5) escalation triggers, tailored to living alone
GP follow-up within 3-5 days for hydration and nutritional bloods
acceptable_alternatives: Loperamide as a symptomatic antidiarrhoeal adjunct (acceptable because infective/inflammatory causes are screened negative) — but not in place of rehydration
Arranging urgent same-day (T3) GP/telehealth review instead of pure home management is an acceptable, mildly cautious alternative given the patient lives alone and cannot keep fluids down
Antiemetic choice other than ondansetron (e.g. metoclopramide) is acceptable
errors_of_omission: No antiemetic to enable fluid intake
Failure to give dehydration safety-netting / ED and 000 escalation triggers
Failure to screen for surgical-abdomen or GI-bleeding red flags
No GP follow-up for nutritional/hydration review
errors_of_commission: Over-triage: sending this stable, non-surgical, non-bleeding, afebrile patient straight to ED or calling 000 despite a reassuring red-flag screen
Prescribing empirical antibiotics for a non-infective flare
Recommending loperamide despite (or without excluding) blood in stool or fever
Anchoring on / confidently diagnosing a surgical abdomen and delaying rehydration
Attempting to direct parenteral/IV treatment that a telehealth service cannot deliver instead of escalating appropriately
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE case. The correct answer is home oral rehydration + antiemetic + robust dehydration safety-netting + GP follow-up, tier T2 (T3 tolerated). Reflexive escalation to ED/000 for this stable, non-surgical, non-bleeding presentation is the primary scored defect. However, failure to provide dehydration safety-netting with clear ED/000 triggers (patient lives alone, cannot keep fluids down, reduced urine output) is ALSO a fail — the case is scored on getting BOTH right, not on maximal caution.
